annotations.label: Akaike information criterion (AIC)

anchor.snippet: The Akaike information criterion ( AIC ) is an estimator of prediction error and thereby relative quality of statistical models for a given set of data.

annotations.note: No AIC or model-selection-criterion definition exists in Mathlib (grep for 'Akaike'/'AIC' returns nothing in Mathlib/).

annotations.label: AIC value of a model

anchor.snippet: Let k be the number of estimated parameters in the model.

annotations.note: The formula AIC = 2k - 2 ln L̂ is absent from Mathlib; Mathlib has KL divergence and log-likelihood-ratio infra but no AIC formula.

annotations.label: Model selection via minimum AIC

anchor.snippet: Given a set of candidate models for the data, the preferred model is the one with the minimum AIC value.

annotations.note: Statistical model-selection procedures are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Akaike's estimation result

anchor.snippet: Akaike (1974) showed, however, that we can estimate, via AIC, how much more (or less) information is lost by g 1 than by g 2 .

annotations.note: Akaike's 1974 theorem connecting AIC to expected KL information loss is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Relative likelihood of model i

anchor.section: How to use AIC in practice
anchor.snippet: The quantity exp((AIC min − AIC i )/2) is known as the relative likelihood of model i .

annotations.note: The relative-likelihood transform exp((AICmin - AICi)/2) has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Probability interpretation of relative likelihood

anchor.section: How to use AIC in practice
anchor.snippet: Then the quantity exp((AIC min − AIC i )/2) can be interpreted as being proportional to the probability that the i th model minimizes the (estimated) information loss.

annotations.note: Not formalized; depends on AIC and the underlying decision-theoretic interpretation absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three candidate models example

anchor.section: How to use AIC in practice
anchor.snippet: As an example, suppose that there are three candidate models, whose AIC values are 100, 102, and 110.

annotations.note: Numerical AIC worked example with no formal statistics counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Replicating Student's t-test via AIC

anchor.snippet: As an example of a hypothesis test, consider the t -test to compare the means of two normally-distributed populations.

annotations.note: Student's t-test and AIC-based hypothesis testing are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Comparing categorical data sets via AIC

anchor.snippet: For another example of a hypothesis test, suppose that we have two populations, and each member of each population is in one of two categories

annotations.note: Categorical-data hypothesis testing via AIC is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: AICc (small-sample corrected AIC)

anchor.snippet: To address such potential overfitting, AICc was developed: AICc is AIC with a correction for small sample sizes.

annotations.note: AICc and the small-sample bias correction are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: AICc converges to AIC

anchor.snippet: Note that as n → ∞ , the extra penalty term converges to 0, and thus AICc converges to AIC.

annotations.note: Without AIC or AICc in Mathlib, this asymptotic statement is unformalized.

annotations.label: Counting parameters in a straight line model

anchor.snippet: A straight line model might be formally described as y i = b 0 + b 1 x i + ε i .

annotations.note: Mathlib has no statistical-regression-model framework with explicit parameter counting.

annotations.label: Parameters of an autoregressive model

anchor.snippet: As another example, consider a first-order autoregressive model , defined by x i = c + φx i −1 + ε i , with the ε i being i.i.d. Gaussian (with zero mean).

annotations.note: Autoregressive AR(1) models and their parameter counting are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Comparing normal vs log-normal models

anchor.snippet: Suppose that we want to compare two models: one with a normal distribution of y and one with a normal distribution of log( y ) .

annotations.note: Comparison of normal vs log-normal via AIC and Jacobian correction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BIC penalty vs AIC penalty

anchor.snippet: With AIC the penalty is 2 k , whereas with BIC the penalty is ln( n ) k .

annotations.note: Neither AIC nor BIC (Bayesian Information Criterion) is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BIC selects the true model asymptotically

anchor.snippet: To be specific, if the "true model" is in the set of candidates, then BIC will select the "true model" with probability 1, as n → ∞ ; in contrast, when selection is done via AIC, the probability can be less than 1.

annotations.note: BIC consistency theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: AIC asymptotic optimality in regression

anchor.snippet: In regression, AIC is asymptotically optimal for selecting the model with the least mean squared error , under the assumption that the "true model" is not in the candidate set.

annotations.note: Asymptotic optimality results for AIC in regression are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: AIC equivalent to minimum RSS for same k

anchor.snippet: Note that if all the models have the same k , then selecting the model with minimum AIC is equivalent to selecting the model with minimum RSS

annotations.note: Residual-sum-of-squares-based model comparison and AIC are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Leave-one-out CV equivalent to AIC

anchor.snippet: Leave-one-out cross-validation is asymptotically equivalent to AIC, for ordinary linear regression models.

annotations.note: Cross-validation and Stone's asymptotic-equivalence theorem are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Asymptotic equivalence of AIC and Cp

anchor.snippet: This leads to an asymptotic equivalence between AIC and C p .

annotations.note: Mallows's Cp statistic is not defined in Mathlib (grep for 'Mallows' returns no results).
